content:
{
  "title": "Reflexive Prompting: Self-Contradiction Detection for Factuality in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models frequently hallucinate and produce non-factual outputs, even when given explicit factuality-focused prompts. Existing methods often rely on instructive cues or post-hoc verification, which may not directly engage the model’s internal consistency.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM factuality is critical for safe deployment in knowledge-sensitive domains. Prior work treats hallucination as an output filtering or post-processing problem, leaving the model’s generative process largely unchanged. Prompting methods that activate the model’s own inconsistency detection capabilities may lead to more reliable factuality, especially as models become more self-reflective.",
  "method": "We propose a reflexive prompting method that augments standard factuality prompts with an explicit self-contradiction check: the model is first asked to answer a question, then immediately prompted to generate statements that would contradict its own answer, and finally to assess whether its original answer is contradicted by any known facts. This iterative, within-context contradiction search leverages the LLM’s own reasoning patterns to surface and suppress hallucinations before producing a final answer. The prompt template ensures the model must explicitly reconcile its output with possible counterfacts, encouraging more cautious and fact-grounded responses.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on TruthfulQA, FactCC, and Wikipedia-based QA datasets. Baselines include standard instruction prompting, chain-of-thought prompting, and retrieval-augmented generation. Metrics will include factual accuracy, hallucination rate, and calibration of self-contradiction detection. Human and automated evaluations will measure whether contradiction-aware prompting reduces hallucinations and improves trustworthiness."
}
